title: Brown soda bread
subtitle: Wholemeal soda bread, ready in under an hour
excerpt: Brown soda bread uses bicarbonate of soda and buttermilk in place of yeast. Wholemeal flour gives it a nutty, slightly dense crumb and a thick, earthy crust. No kneading, no proving: mix, shape, score, and bake. Best eaten the day it is made, cut in thick wedges with salted butter.
sourceNotes: Irish soda bread is documented from at least the 1830s, when bicarbonate of soda became widely available to domestic cooks. The brown or wholemeal version, using coarser flour, appears in Florence White's Good Things in England (1932) and in Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. The cross scored on the top has both practical and traditional origins: it helps the bread bake through to the centre and allows steam to escape.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely, then wrap and freeze for up to 1 month. Defrost at room temperature for 2 to 3 hours.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Brown soda bread does not improve with time in the way yeasted loaves do. Bake on the day you want to eat it.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; bake for 30 to 35 minutes until the base sounds hollow

content.text: Brown soda bread is one of the fastest loaves you can make. The whole process from bowl to oven takes around 10 minutes, with no mixing machine and no waiting for yeast to work. The bicarbonate of soda reacts with the acid in the buttermilk the moment they meet, producing bubbles that lift the loaf. Work quickly: mix until the dough just comes together, shape into a round, and bake at once.

Heat the oven to 200 degrees C fan. Line a baking tray with baking paper or lightly flour it. Combine 350 g wholemeal flour, 100 g plain flour, 1.5 tsp bicarbonate of soda, and 1 tsp salt in a large bowl and stir to mix. Make a well in the centre and pour in 350 ml buttermilk. Stir quickly with a round-bladed knife until the dough comes together into a rough, shaggy ball. Do not overwork it: a few dry patches at the base of the bowl are fine. Turn out onto a floured surface and gather into a round about 18 cm across and 5 cm deep. Handle lightly.

Transfer to the prepared tray. Scatter rolled oats over the top if using. Score a deep cross over the top, cutting down to about 2 cm from the base. The cross allows steam to escape and ensures the centre cooks through. Bake for 30 to 35 minutes until the loaf is well browned and the base sounds hollow when you tap it firmly. Leave to cool on a rack for at least 10 minutes before cutting: cutting a hot soda bread compresses the crumb.

items.problem: Bread is dense and doughy inside
items.solution: Either the cross was not cut deep enough to allow heat to the centre, or the oven temperature was too low. The cross should go almost to the base. Check the oven temperature with a thermometer.

items.problem: Bread is very crumbly and dry
items.solution: The dough was too dry. Wholemeal flour absorbs more liquid than white. Next time, add the buttermilk gradually and stop when the dough just comes together into a rough ball, or increase by 20 to 30 ml.
